text: The surgeon begins the dissection medially, approximately six to eight centimeters above the inguinal hernia defect, using a fenestrated bipolar instrument. The opened area measures about two centimeters from end to end.
The surgeon continues the dissection laterally from the initial medial starting point, maintaining a horizontal approach to avoid drifting inferiorly during the procedure.
The surgeon works medially in the retro-rectus space, carefully dissecting down to the symphysis pubis using a fenestrated bipolar instrument, while avoiding the bladder in the area.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection using a fenestrated bipolar, angling it downward to catch and pull the edge of the peritoneum.
The surgeon brings the medial and lateral dissections together, converging at the hernia sac in the inguinal region.
The surgeon works just above the hernia sac, completing the dissection in this area before proceeding inferiorly, where nerves and vessels are visible and require careful navigation.
The surgeon carefully reduces the indirect hernia defect, utilizing clear visualization to ensure precision in the procedure.

The surgeon completes the dissection and enters the fourth stage, focusing on the inferior portion. The dissection extends one to two centimeters below the symphysis pubis and two centimeters across the midline to ensure the mesh covers the area adequately.

The surgeon navigates the dissection over the femoral artery and vein, ensuring the space is cleared for the mesh to lie securely in the groove inferiorly, preventing the peritoneum from sliding underneath.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the vessels and cord structures near the femoral canal, using scissors to gently pull tissue away from the femoral vein.

The surgeon places the mesh flat in the groove created during dissection, ensuring it covers the central portion of the defect. The entire myopectineal orifice is exposed, facilitating easy placement of the 12 by 16 cm mesh.
